questions.question: A 32 KB, 8-way set-associative cache with 64-byte lines has how many index bits in a 32-bit address?
options: 4 bits
6 bits
8 bits
10 bits
questions.explanation: Number of sets = 32768 / (8 × 64) = 64. log₂(64) = 6 index bits.

questions.question: In a direct-mapped cache, two frequently accessed addresses that map to the same set will cause:
options: Cold misses on every access
Capacity misses that increase with associativity
Conflict misses — each access evicts the other
No penalty because the tag distinguishes them
questions.explanation: Conflict misses occur when two blocks compete for the same cache line. Increasing associativity (e.g., 2-way) lets both coexist.

questions.question: In the MESI protocol, a cache line in the Exclusive (E) state is:
options: Dirty and held by multiple caches
Clean and the only copy in any cache
Invalid until a bus transaction upgrades it
Shared with at least one other cache
questions.explanation: E means the line is clean (matches memory) and this cache holds the only copy. A local write can silently upgrade E to M without a bus transaction.

questions.question: Which write policy updates main memory only when a dirty cache line is evicted?
options: Write-through
No-write-allocate
Write-back
Write-invalidate
questions.explanation: Write-back marks the line dirty on writes and defers the memory update until eviction. This reduces bus traffic compared to write-through, which writes to memory on every store.

questions.question: Why do modern CPUs use multiple levels of cache (L1, L2, L3)?
options: To reduce the total cost of the system
To balance speed and capacity: small fast caches close to the CPU, larger slower caches further away
Because a single large cache would be too fast
To comply with industry standards
questions.explanation: Memory hierarchy exploits locality: L1 is small (~64KB) but extremely fast (1-4 cycles), L2 is larger (~256KB-1MB) but slower, and L3 is largest (~8-64MB) but slowest. This gives the illusion of fast, large memory — most accesses hit in L1, but L2/L3 catch the rest.
